name: SCAMPER
origin: Eberle, B. (1996). Scamper: Games for Imagination Development. Prufrock Press.
steps_summary: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse — apply 7 operators to problem statement
sources: [1] Eberle (1996); [2] Osborn, A.F. (1953). Applied Imagination. Scribner.

name: Five Whys
origin: Ohno, T. (1988). Toyota Production System. Productivity Press.
steps_summary: Ask 'Why?' 5 times iteratively from initial symptom until root cause is identified
sources: [1] Ohno (1988) — Toyota Production System; [2] Serrat, O. (2017). The Five Whys Technique. ADB Knowledge Solutions.

name: Design Thinking
origin: Brown, T. (2008). Design Thinking. Harvard Business Review, 86(6), 84-92.
best_for: human-centered problem framing for vague or wicked problems
steps_summary: Empathize → Define → Ideate — diverge first, then converge to defined problem statement
sources: [1] Brown (2008) HBR; [2] IDEO (2015). The Field Guide to Human-Centered Design.

name: PICO Framework
origin: Richardson, W.S., et al. (1995). The well-built clinical question: a key to evidence-based decisions. ACP Journal Club, 123(3), A12-A13.
steps_summary: Define Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome. Extension: PICOC (+Context), SPIDER (Sample, Phenomenon of Interest, Design, Evaluation, Research type)
sources: [1] Richardson et al. (1995) ACP Journal Club; [2] Cooke, A., et al. (2012). Beyond PICO: the SPIDER tool. Qualitative Health Research, 22(10), 1435-1443.

name: PRISMA-P 2015
origin: Moher, D., et al. (2015). Preferred reporting items for systematic review and meta-analysis protocols (PRISMA-P) 2015 statement. Systematic Reviews, 4(1), 1.
best_for: developing a review protocol before evidence collection
steps_summary: Define eligibility criteria, information sources, search strategy, study records management, data items, outcomes, risk of bias, synthesis plan
sources: [1] Moher et al. (2015) Systematic Reviews; [2] Page, M.J., et al. (2021). PRISMA 2020 statement. BMJ, 372, n71.

name: Systematic Review Protocol
origin: Kitchenham, B., & Charters, S. (2007). Guidelines for performing Systematic Literature Reviews in Software Engineering. EBSE Technical Report EBSE-2007-01.
best_for: search strategy design and source taxonomy mapping
steps_summary: Formulate research questions → define search string → select digital libraries → define inclusion/exclusion → quality assessment checklist → data extraction form → synthesis method
sources: [1] Kitchenham & Charters (2007) EBSE TR; [2] Higgins, J.P.T., & Green, S. (2011). Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions. Wiley.

name: OSINT Cycle
origin: NATO (2001). Open Source Intelligence Handbook; Williams, H.J., & Blum, I. (2018). Defining Second Generation OSINT. RAND Corporation.
best_for: multi-source intelligence collection from open/public sources
steps_summary: Direction → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination. Sources: surface web, deep web, social media, academic databases, news, market reports
sources: [1] NATO OSINT Handbook (2001); [2] Williams & Blum (2018) RAND Corp; [3] OSINT Framework (osintframework.com).

name: Cochrane Systematic Search
origin: Higgins, J.P.T., & Green, S. (2011). Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions, Chapter 4: Searching for and selecting studies. Wiley.
steps_summary: Design search strategy → select databases → run queries → deduplicate → screen by title/abstract → screen full-text → extract data
sources: [1] Higgins & Green (2011) Cochrane Handbook; [2] Lefebvre, C., et al. (2022). Chapter 4: Searching for and selecting studies. Cochrane Handbook v6.3.

name: Thematic Synthesis
origin: Thomas, J., & Harden, A. (2008). Methods for the thematic synthesis of qualitative research in systematic reviews. BMC Medical Research Methodology, 8(1), 45.
best_for: integrating findings from heterogeneous sources into themes
steps_summary: Line-by-line coding of findings → develop descriptive themes → generate analytical themes → map themes to research questions
sources: [1] Thomas & Harden (2008) BMC Med Res Methodol; [2] Barnett-Page, E., & Thomas, J. (2009). Methods for the synthesis of qualitative research. BMC Med Res Methodol, 9, 59.

name: Framework Synthesis
origin: Carroll, C., et al. (2013). 'Best fit' framework synthesis: refining the method. BMC Medical Research Methodology, 13(1), 37.
best_for: synthesizing evidence against an a-priori framework
steps_summary: Select 'best fit' framework from literature → index findings against framework → thematic analysis of data not captured by framework → produce refined framework
sources: [1] Carroll et al. (2013) BMC Med Res Methodol; [2] Booth, A., & Carroll, C. (2015). How to build up the actionable knowledge base. Health Info Libr J.

name: Technology Assessment
origin: U.S. Government Accountability Office (2021). Technology Assessment Design Handbook. GAO-21-347G.
best_for: assessing technology maturity, feasibility, and policy implications
steps_summary: Describe technology → assess status/feasibility (TRL) → assess opportunities/challenges → analyze policy implications → review alternatives → produce structured report
sources: [1] GAO (2021) TA Design Handbook GAO-21-347G; [2] Pahl, G., et al. (2007). Engineering Design: A Systematic Approach. Springer.

name: Feasibility Study Methodology
origin: USACE (2023). Feasibility Report Format and Content Guide. ER 1105-2-100; IMARC Engineering (2025).
steps_summary: Problem statement → objectives/constraints → alternatives analysis → technical assessment → cost-benefit → risk analysis → environmental review → recommendation
sources: [1] USACE (2023) Feasibility Report Guide; [2] PMI (2021). A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 7th ed.

name: GRADE
origin: Guyatt, G.H., et al. (2008). GRADE: an emerging consensus on rating quality of evidence and strength of recommendations. BMJ, 336(7650), 924-926.
best_for: rating quality/certainty of evidence for claims
steps_summary: Assess risk of bias, imprecision, inconsistency, indirectness, publication bias → rate as High/Moderate/Low/Very Low → grade recommendation strength
sources: [1] Guyatt et al. (2008) BMJ; [2] Balshem, H., et al. (2011). GRADE guidelines: 3. Rating the quality of evidence. J Clin Epidemiol, 64(4), 401-406.

name: CASP Checklists
origin: Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (2018). CASP Checklists. casp-uk.net/casp-tools-checklists. UK NHS.
best_for: structured critical appraisal of individual source quality
steps_summary: 10-12 questions per study type: (A) Are results valid? (B) What are the results? (C) Will results help locally? — Systematic Review, Qualitative, RCT, Cohort, Case-Control variants
sources: [1] CASP (2018) UK NHS; [2] Hannes, K., et al. (2010). A comparative analysis of three online appraisal instruments. Qual Health Res, 20(12).

name: AMSTAR 2
origin: Shea, B.J., et al. (2017). AMSTAR 2: a critical appraisal tool for systematic reviews that include randomised or non-randomised studies. BMJ, 358, j4008.
best_for: assessing methodological quality of completed review
steps_summary: 16-item checklist: PICO components, protocol registration, search comprehensiveness, study selection duplication, data extraction, risk of bias, synthesis methods, publication bias, conflict of interest
sources: [1] Shea et al. (2017) BMJ j4008; [2] Whiting, P., et al. (2016). ROBIS: tool to assess risk of bias. J Clin Epidemiol, 69, 225-234.

name: PRISMA 2020 Checklist
origin: Page, M.J., et al. (2021). The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ, 372, n71.
steps_summary: 27-item checklist covering title, abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion, other information — each item must be reported
sources: [1] Page et al. (2021) BMJ n71; [2] Liberati, A., et al. (2009). The PRISMA statement. PLoS Med, 6(7), e1000100.

name: FAIR Data Principles
origin: Wilkinson, M.D., et al. (2016). The FAIR Guiding Principles for scientific data management and stewardship. Scientific Data, 3, 160018.
best_for: making research artifacts Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
steps_summary: Assign persistent identifier (DOI) → register metadata → use open formats → link data to sources → document provenance → preserve in accessible repository
sources: [1] Wilkinson et al. (2016) Scientific Data/Nature; [2] GO FAIR Initiative (go-fair.org).
